questions.question: What is the distant past called when there was no paper, language, or written word?
questions.explanation: The distant past when there was no paper or language or the written word is called prehistory or prehistoric times.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which of the following were the oldest art forms practised by human beings to express themselves?
questions.explanation: Painting and drawing were the oldest art forms practised by early human beings to express themselves, using cave walls as their canvas.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What is the early development period of human beings commonly known as?
questions.explanation: The prehistoric period in the early development of human beings is commonly known as the Old Stone Age or the Palaeolithic Age.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: In which period do we see a proliferation of artistic activities in prehistoric times?
questions.explanation: While it is unknown if Lower Palaeolithic people produced art, by the Upper Palaeolithic times we see a clear proliferation of artistic activities.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: When were the earliest paintings in India reported to have been created?
questions.explanation: In India, the earliest paintings have been reported from the Upper Palaeolithic times.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: In which year was the first discovery of rock paintings made in India?
questions.explanation: The first discovery of rock paintings was made in India in the years 1867-68.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Who made the first discovery of rock paintings in India?
questions.explanation: The first discovery of rock paintings in India was made by an archaeologist named Archibold Carlleyle.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: The discovery of rock paintings in India happened how many years before the discovery of Altamira in Spain?
questions.explanation: Archibold Carlleyle discovered the rock paintings twelve years before the famous discovery of Altamira in Spain.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which of the following archaeologists was among the early discoverers of rock painting sites in the Indian sub-continent?
questions.explanation: Cockburn, Anderson, Mitra, and Ghosh were the early archaeologists who discovered a large number of sites in the Indian sub-continent.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: On the banks of which river are the Lakhudiyar rock shelters situated?
questions.explanation: The rock shelters on the banks of the River Suyal at Lakhudiyar bear these prehistoric paintings.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What does the word 'Lakhudiyar' literally mean?
questions.explanation: The term Lakhudiyar literally translates to one lakh caves.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the broad categories of paintings found at Lakhudiyar?
questions.explanation: The paintings here are divided into three categories: man, animal, and geometric patterns. Floral patterns are absent.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: How are humans represented in the Lakhudiyar rock paintings?
questions.explanation: Humans are distinctly represented in stick-like forms at the Lakhudiyar site.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which of these animals is a main motif at the Lakhudiyar caves?
questions.explanation: A long-snouted animal, a fox, and a multiple legged lizard are noted as the main animal motifs at Lakhudiyar.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What colors were primarily used for the paintings at Lakhudiyar?
questions.explanation: The paintings at Lakhudiyar are done using white, black, and red ochre.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: In the superimposition of paintings at Lakhudiyar, which color represents the earliest layer?
questions.explanation: The earliest paintings are in black; over these are red ochre paintings, and the last group comprises white paintings.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What type of artistic findings have been reported from Kashmir?
questions.explanation: From Kashmir, two slabs with engravings have been reported by archaeologists.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which stone provided a suitable canvas for Neolithic men in Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh?
questions.explanation: The granite rocks of Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh provided highly suitable canvases to the Neolithic man for his paintings.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which of the following is a famous rock painting site in South India?
questions.explanation: There are several sites in South India, but the more famous among them are Kupgallu, Piklihal, and Tekkalkota.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which motif is rarely found in the rock paintings of South India?
questions.explanation: The subjects depicted include bulls, elephants, sambhars, sheep, and tridents, but rarely vegetal motifs.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: The richest rock paintings in India are reported from which mountain ranges?
questions.explanation: The richest rock paintings are reported from the Vindhya ranges of Madhya Pradesh and their Kaimurean extensions.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Where is the largest and most spectacular rock-shelter located in India?
questions.explanation: The largest and most spectacular rock-shelter in India is located in the Vindhya hills at Bhimbetka.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: How far is Bhimbetka located from Bhopal?
questions.explanation: Bhimbetka is geographically located forty-five kilometres south of Bhopal.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Approximately how many rock shelters have been found in the Bhimbetka area?
questions.explanation: The Bhimbetka area contains approximately eight hundred rock shelters spread over ten square kilometres.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Out of the rock shelters discovered at Bhimbetka, how many bear paintings?
questions.explanation: Out of the eight hundred rock shelters at Bhimbetka, five hundred of them bear paintings.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Who discovered the caves of Bhimbetka in 1957-58?
questions.explanation: The caves of Bhimbetka were discovered in 1957-58 by the eminent archaeologist V.S. Wakankar.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: The rock art of Bhimbetka has been classified into how many historical periods?
questions.explanation: Based on style, technique, and superimposition, the drawings and paintings are categorised into seven historical periods.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which period does Period I of the Bhimbetka paintings belong to?
questions.explanation: Period I of the Bhimbetka historical classification belongs to the Upper Palaeolithic era.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What colors were primarily used in the Upper Palaeolithic phase of Bhimbetka paintings?
questions.explanation: The paintings of the Upper Palaeolithic phase are primarily linear representations in green and dark red.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: In the Upper Palaeolithic phase at Bhimbetka, what did the green paintings typically depict?
questions.explanation: During this phase, the green paintings specifically depicted dancers.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: In the Upper Palaeolithic phase at Bhimbetka, what did the red paintings typically depict?
questions.explanation: While green was used for dancers, the red paintings were used to depict hunters.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which historical period has the largest number of paintings at Bhimbetka?
questions.explanation: The largest number of paintings at the Bhimbetka site belong to Period II, which is the Mesolithic period.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which theme predominates the paintings of the Mesolithic Period at Bhimbetka?
questions.explanation: During the Mesolithic period, although themes are multiple, hunting scenes heavily predominate the artwork.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: In Mesolithic hunting scenes, primitive men are sometimes shown with what tools besides weapons to catch animals?
questions.explanation: In some Mesolithic paintings, primitive men are shown equipped with traps and snares to catch animals.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: How are humans depicted stylistically in Mesolithic paintings as compared to animals?
options: Humans were naturalistic, animals were stylistic
Animals were naturalistic, humans were stylistic
questions.explanation: The artists painted animals in a naturalistic style, whereas humans were depicted only in a stylistic manner.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What common theme involving children is depicted in the Mesolithic paintings?
questions.explanation: Children are often painted engaging in activities like running, jumping, and playing.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Apart from human and animal figures, what specific prints are found in many of the rock-shelters?
questions.explanation: In many of the rock-shelters, artists left hand prints, fist prints, and dots made by their fingertips.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What were the favourite colours of the artists of Bhimbetka?
questions.explanation: While they used many colours, white and red were the absolute favourite colours of the Bhimbetka artists.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: From which material did the prehistoric artists obtain their red colour?
questions.explanation: The red colour was obtained by grinding haematite, commonly known as geru in India.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What stone provided the green colour for the prehistoric artists?
questions.explanation: The green colour was derived from a green variety of a stone called chalcedony.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What mineral was likely used to make the white paint in prehistoric times?
questions.explanation: It is believed that the white colour might have been made out of limestone.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: To make paint, the powdered minerals were mixed with water and what other possible substances?
questions.explanation: The mineral powder was mixed with water and thick or sticky substances such as animal fat, gum, or tree resin.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: What material did prehistoric artists use to make their brushes?
questions.explanation: The artists made their painting brushes out of plant fibre.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Why is it believed that the colours of these rock paintings have survived thousands of years?
options: Because of the chemical reaction of the oxide present on the surface of the rocks
They were continuously retouched by later generations
They were protected by advanced varnishes
questions.explanation: The colours have remained intact largely due to the chemical reaction of the oxide present on the surface of the rocks.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Why did early humans likely paint high up on uncomfortable parts of the rock shelters?
options: For the paintings to be noticed from a distance
Because the lower walls were too rough
questions.explanation: Paintings made in high, uncomfortable positions were likely placed there so people could easily notice them from a distance.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: In the hunting scenes depicted at Bhimbetka, what emotion might a group of dancing men feel when an animal is shown dying?
options: A sense of power over the animals
questions.explanation: Paintings showing men dancing while an animal is in the agony of death might have given man a sense of power over the animals they faced.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: How are the animals generally represented in the prehistoric paintings regarding their physical state?
questions.explanation: The artists tended to show the animals as more youthful and majestic than they perhaps actually were in reality.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: At Bhimbetka, what is a notable feature regarding the placement of new paintings over time?
options: They never painted in the same spot twice
They carved away old paintings before making new ones
A new painting is often painted on top of an older painting
They strictly separated paintings by historical period
questions.explanation: At many rock-art sites, including Bhimbetka, it is common to see a new painting painted directly on top of an older painting.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: In some places at Bhimbetka, up to how many layers of paintings can be found one on top of another?
questions.explanation: There are places at Bhimbetka where as many as 20 distinct layers of paintings are stacked one on top of another.
questions.subject: Fine Arts

questions.question: Which of the following is considered a possible reason for early humans superimposing paintings on top of older ones?
options: They ran out of space in the cave
The area may have been considered sacred and used by different generations
They wanted to hide the older paintings from enemies
questions.explanation: Superimposition may have occurred because certain spaces were considered sacred or special, leading different generations to use the exact same area.
questions.subject: Fine Arts
